text: I know these films are usually safe but this was safe up to 11. I can't remember any scenes that showed us any of Jackson's character flaws even without going near the allegations. Felt like they over used Joe Jackson, because he's dead he's not going to say anything and it was the easy angle. At points he was such a caricature that I thought he was going to start twirling his mustache. They could have showed some conflict with the brothers or in the studio.
label: analysis
notes: Specific structural critique — Joe Jackson caricature, missing character flaws, missed conflict opportunities

text: I love that Michael didn't have a single character flaw. Just a perfect, pure-hearted saint whose only hobbies were visiting sick children and reading Peter Pan to his horrifying CGI pet monkey. He also ended racism, I think? Powerful film.
label: analysis

text: They should have cut nearly everything Jackson 5 related, the miniseries did it 10 times better. Honestly, the whole movie should have just been the making of Thriller

text: This was the most blatant example of propaganda in a movie I've had the pleasure of seeing in a while. MJ is literally depicted as a perfect angel. It's a movie designed for a crazy fan base to cope with the fact that the Jesus Christ figure marketed to them as children was actually that way in real life. This is less Bohemian Rhapsody and more Passion of the Christ.
label: analysis
notes: Specific framing critique with a named comparison

text: The Bohemian Rhapsody style editing definitely made the second half worse to watch. Also, wished we got the full Motown 25 and Grammy stuff tackled instead of just 5 minute montages. Super minor nitpick but I didn't like that PYT didn't get any time in the sun off Thriller.
label: analysis
notes: Names a specific editing style comparison and specific omissions by name

text: The movie dragged because his Dad hung around like a pungent fart and it kept delaying the inevitable. For the sake of the film it's just not all that interesting watching Michael read Peter Pan in 4 different scenes and repeatedly saying 'I need to take my own path' almost the whole entire movie.
label: analysis
notes: Specific pacing critique — names the Peter Pan repetition and counts the scenes

text: michael states his motives. either joseph goes against it, or mother and bill support it. recreate the iconic moment without any flair, but still expect an audience reaction. have a musical montage that is horribly edited. make sure branca is there for some reason. repeat until the end.
label: analysis

text: For a biopic that heavily embraces the cheese, it had a lot of missed opportunities to go further. Loved the 'I have to be here to receive these ideas. Otherwise, God will give them to Prince' line. But Michael watching the gang wars news report workshopping Beat It transitions quietly into the limo with no 'eureka' moment. After Mike Myers threatens the MTV producer to put Thriller on air, the scene moves on to MJ on a limo without the middle step of achieving the result.
label: analysis
notes: Names two specific scenes where the transition misses an opportunity — verifiable

text: I wish they focused more on Michael's creativity as an artist and less on how much of a jerk his dad was. After a certain point we totally got the idea of his shitty dad and I was ready to move on, but it lingered the whole time. I would have liked to see Michael learning the moonwalk for the first time, and more about him writing his monster hits.
label: analysis

text: this movie really felt like if you got AI to write a summary of michael jacksons life in dot point and then just shot scenes to match that. i dont think ive seen a more sterile music biopic in my life which is an insane thing to feel for arguably one of the greatest performers and entertainers of all time.
label: analysis
notes: Specific characterization of the film's structure and its irony given the subject

text: I saw this film tonight at the Grand Lake theatre in Oakland at a mostly Black hardcore Michael fandom. It was a pleasure and a privilege. Everyone jeering at Michael's Dad like he was a villain in a cartoon, cheering for Michael's power moves. This film is significantly recreated performances, and a lot of the family drama is presented in stilted, operatic formats. I could not possibly care less that this almost certainly did not happen. It was a perfect climax to the drama. This is not a biopic in the traditional sense. This was a Jackson-estate sanctioned kinetic hagiography to Michael Jackson, The King of Pop. See it with a crowd if you can.
label: analysis
notes: Explains the RT/audience score gap through the lens of a specific screening experience — describes what the film is designed to do

text: I think the biggest issue is the fact that Fuqua doesn't have the chops to tackle a music biopic and the Jackson Estate would never let this be remotely interesting. It's a fluff piece and not a particularly good one. Kinda wish the Better Man team made this instead.

text: First of all, I can't believe they put a Bikini Kill needle drop in a Pixar movie. text: My favorite is Diane the shark, she's such a scene stealer. The vicious looking shark but with Vanessa Bayer demeanor is a win combination. text: I can't get over the fact that the movie tries to give King George the higher moral ground against 'squishing' while simultaneously having him rule a kingdom that allows some of his subjects eat others without remorse. text: King George's reaction when he thought Mable was captured and killed really touched me. He walked towards Mable's beaver looking all sad and made those sad noises. That's my favorite scene of the movie.

text: The transition from seeing King George from the animal POV and the human POV was so subtle yet so powerful, easily my favorite underrated moment of the movie. When we first see him from the human perspective as he cradles Mabel's beaver body, it's just a simple camera perspective change but really affects how you view him, from the wise and sensible beaver to the poor confused animal trying to make sense of human tech
label: analysis

text: Our ACTUAL villain Titus is introduced WAY too late to land as a satisfying antagonist. His motivations are sketched in so quickly they barely register; I guess he was sad about his mom dying? But then suddenly once they have him bound and gagged the focus switches to killing the randos at the press conference. That said: major props for the creep factor of the fake Jerry robot suit. That thing was nightmare fuel. I genuinely can't believe we have a Pixar film where the villain's plan involves liquefying brains with weaponised soundwaves.
label: analysis
notes: Specific structural critique of Titus's narrative function and a named scene observation

text: On the highway construction plot point - it's nice to see a movie tackle that issue since so much of the US is being plagued by insane car dependency and endless highway expansion right now. text: What was so frustrating to me was the way the movie would present some great feminist ideas and then shrink away afraid to commit to them. When The Bride awakens she's her own spirit, not willing to be defined by Frank's desire for a mate. But at the club she gets assaulted and he comes to her rescue and she then follows him around like a lost puppy. Through the whole second act he gaslights her about their relationship, having sex with her under false pretenses. But after he comes clean she comes back to him again. And then there's the female revolution, which lasts one scene and never goes anywhere.
label: analysis
notes: Names specific scenes and tracks the arc across acts — all verifiable

text: It bothered me so much that this was supposed to be a movie about female empowerment, but the actual title character had no agency. First she was possessed by a ghost without her consent, then brought back to life without her consent, then essentially tricked into a relationship and gaslit. She even says 'I didn't ask for any of this!', but the movie didn't seem to have enough self awareness to recognize that. Her whole identity is just 'The Bride' aka being married to someone, and she doesn't even give herself an actual name. That is so backwards for what the story is trying to advocate.
label: analysis
notes: Quotes a specific line of dialogue and traces the agency argument through the whole film

text: I felt that any positive feminist ideas were undermined by the director immediately turning Mary Shelley into an agent of their own self-indulgence. Mary Shelley didn't want to write the enormously profound, layered, ahead of its time Frankenstein. What she actually wanted was to write a shallow mess and periodically show up to spout cringey garbled nonsense like a drunk aunt.
label: analysis
notes: Specific critique of the Mary Shelley narrative device

text: Truly a bizarre mix of tones.

text: Am I crazy or was the Dr. implying she turned her husband into the cat? She mentions at the start of the movie, when talking with Frankie about her work, that she also reinvigorated cats. text: The cat is credited as George The Cat in the credits. To me that confirmed this theory.

text: People acting super shocked and horrified seeing Frank read as so hollow to me because this is occurring in major cities post-WWI. text: What I will say is I really liked the Puttin' on the Ritz dance sequence. That felt like the moment when the movie was fully executing its potential. text: The 'female astronaut' line bugs me extra, because it breaks the setting extremely far. The movie's set in the 1930s, the jet engine hadn't been invented yet, let alone the word astronaut. If it was an intentional break with the setting like the nightclub, then I don't think it was a good choice. The movie looks great when it sticks to its 1930s setting.

text: Once Mary Shelley started to talk about how she was never able to write the book she truly wanted to write and decided to OC herself into the movie as The Bride's tourettes, I knew we were in for a mess.

text: If you're gonna invoke the voice of a dead author to claim that you're telling the one true adaptation of their work, then you had better be about to drop a fucking masterpiece. That's stepping back from home plate to call your shot. If you do that and then don't hit a home run, then you're a hack.
label: analysis

text: It's a beautiful movie and well made and Ryan Gosling is great and Rocky is adorable…but I really didn't like it. Characters constantly reassuring the audience that things are still fun. Tension being undermined quickly with a joke. Dialogue that often sounds optimized for audience enjoyment rather than realism. It feels like a Marvel movie at times. Like this exchange: Grace: 'The Sky is slightly on fire' Rocky: 'Words of encouragement'. They're on a dangerous mission and the two of them have that bit of banter. I don't mind it in a Marvel movie, but this could be such a better and more profound movie without it.
label: analysis
notes: Names specific structural complaint with a quoted example — checkable

text: This was one thing that bugged me a bit. The beltway Jerry is obsessed with is shown repeatedly to be useless — 'connecting Beaverton to itself', 'commutes up to four minutes shorter' — and there's no good reason for it except to give him something to brag about. One of the main messages of the movie is the importance of finding common ground and communicating in good faith with people you disagree with, but it undercuts it a little by having one side of the central disagreement just kinda not have anything redeeming about it.
label: analysis
notes: Specific thematic critique with quoted examples from the film

text: I don't think I've ever walked out of a cinema this frustrated. By leaning so hard into portraying him as naive and almost childlike, the film strips away everything that actually made him compelling: his ambition, his creativity, his contradictions. The Beat It scene especially annoyed me — with the whole L.A. gang conflict setup, it felt like they were about to show him stepping out of his bubble. Instead it just turns into a random dance sequence and then gets dropped completely.

text: Lots and lots of thoughts on this movie. When Charlie and Rachel talk about what they did they use the excuse of being kids but when it comes to Emma they don't give her that same grace. Also Charlie cyber bullying someone so much that they possibly had to move away just gets glossed over. My takeaway was how quickly people want to categorize others and can't live in the complicated gray area we all exist in. The DJ thing is an interesting microcosm — they never would've fired her had they never seen her on the street. But they fixated on it and that one act became who she was instead of considering all of the other evidence they had of her being a normal person.
label: analysis

text: I really appreciate the analysis in the second paragraph. Also, from a legal standpoint, Emma is the only one who can't be charged with harassment or abuse. This movie gave me the same feeling as Thomas Vinterberg's The Celebration. Just a small event or conversation that tilts the entire movie on its axis and every frame moving forward isn't quite sitting right.

text: Just got out of this film (Australia). SIGH. Some decent set pieces. Some of the nastiest, most mean-spirited kills in the franchise. My obsession with Scream comes down to the pairing of a high octane slasher with a stimulating, satisfying whodunnit. These elements (especially the latter) have been sorely lacking in the new era IMO, and are nowhere to be seen in this instalment. I'm happy to see Sidney back, but the new characters are very poorly developed (couldn't even tell you most of their names) and the returning characters are, once again, barely utilised. The first 'bad' Scream film in my opinion. We had a good run.
